phase_overview.goal: Build core algorithmic thinking (arrays, hashing, sorting, searching, stacks, queues, heaps) while simultaneously launching Statistics Tier 1A (descriptive stats, probability, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing). Six target problems solved. Daily external-platform practice begins.
phase_overview.daily_budget: 11 hours — hybrid schedule (5h DSA / 3h Stats / 2h Problem Practice / 1h Evening Review).
phase_overview.problem_solving: LeetCode daily starting Day 22 (Easy → Medium progression). StrataScratch (continued from Day 18). DataLemur Python + probability questions.

new_libraries_in_scope.library: scipy.stats (bernoulli, binom, poisson, norm, expon, bootstrap, ttest_*, shapiro, levene, chisquare, chi2_contingency, fisher_exact, mode)

week.title: Arrays, Hashing + Descriptive Stats & Probability
week.objective: Master array manipulation patterns (two-pointer, sliding window, prefix sums) and hash map techniques. Begin statistics with descriptive stats (mean/median/mode, variance/std, quantiles) and probability foundations (axioms, conditional, Bayes, Law of Total Probability). Start daily LeetCode practice.

day.title: Big-O Notation & Array Foundations
day.topics: Big-O notation, space complexity, Python list internals, array iteration, two-pointer pattern (introduction), descriptive statistics Part 1 (mean, median, mode)

concepts.concept: Big-O notation: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2ⁿ), O(n!)
Space complexity: auxiliary space vs total space, in-place algorithms
How to analyze code: count operations, identify dominant terms, drop constants
Python list internals: dynamic arrays, amortized O(1) append, O(n) insert at index 0
Array problems introduction: iterate, search, two-pointer pattern (start)
Arithmetic mean, weighted mean, trimmed mean — when to use each
Median: why it's robust, odd vs even length, relationship to mean (skewness)
Mode: when useful (categorical data), multimodal distributions

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Big-O theory (all complexity classes), space complexity, how to analyze code, Python list internals (dynamic arrays, amortized O(1) append). Array problems introduction + two-pointer pattern (intro). 5 problems: find max/min in single pass, check for duplicate elements, reverse an array in-place, move all zeros to end preserving order (LC #283-style), find missing number in 0..n (LC #268-style).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Descriptive Statistics Part 1: arithmetic/weighted/trimmed mean (when to use each), median robustness, mode for categorical data. Python implementation from scratch (no numpy), then verify with np.mean/np.median/scipy.stats.mode. Visualize: skewed dataset, plot histogram, mark mean/median/mode. Interview insight: "When would you use median instead of mean?" — the outlier story.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode Easy: 3 array problems. Two Sum (LC #1) brute-force today (hash-map version on Day 25). Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock (LC #121-style). Contains Duplicate (LC #217-style).
block.platform: LeetCode Easy — Two Sum (#1 brute-force), Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock (#121), Contains Duplicate (#217) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Big-O flashcards for common complexities. Stats: quiz yourself on mean/median/mode distinctions.

day.notes: First day of hybrid DSA + Stats schedule. Two Sum solved brute-force only; hash-map version is Day 25's lead-in. scipy.stats.mode is a verification-only usage — first mention of scipy.stats before Day 29's formal entry to scope.

day.title: Two-Pointer Pattern + Variance & Standard Deviation
day.topics: Two-pointer technique (same-direction, opposite-direction templates), variance, standard deviation, Bessel's correction, coefficient of variation

concepts.concept: Two-pointer technique: theory, when to apply (sorted arrays, finding pairs), templates
Two-pointer variants: same-direction vs opposite-direction
Population vs sample variance (n vs n-1 divisor)
Bessel's correction: why n-1 gives unbiased estimation, derivation intuition
Standard deviation: square root of variance, units match data, 68-95-99.7 rule (preview)
Coefficient of variation: comparing variability across scales

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Two-pointer technique theory + opposite-direction and same-direction templates. 8 problems: Two Sum on sorted array (two pointers), Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array (LC #26-style), Container With Most Water (LC #11-style), Valid Palindrome (LC #125-style), Three Sum (LC #15-style) sort + two pointers, merge two sorted arrays in-place, Squares of a Sorted Array (LC #977-style), Dutch National Flag / Sort Colors (LC #75-style).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Variance: population vs sample (n vs n-1 divisor), Bessel's correction intuition. Standard deviation: √variance interpretation (68-95-99.7 preview), units-match-data. Python: compute variance and std from scratch, compare with np.var(ddof=0) and np.var(ddof=1). Coefficient of variation. Plot distributions with same mean but different std. Exercise: dataset of exam scores, compute all descriptive stats, write a 3-sentence summary a manager would understand.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode Easy: 3 problems. DataLemur: 1 easy data science question.
block.platform: LeetCode Easy (3 two-pointer-adjacent problems); DataLemur (1 Easy) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Review two-pointer templates (same vs opposite direction). Stats: explain Bessel's correction to a non-statistician.

day.notes: Three Sum is first true Medium-level DSA problem of Phase 2. Stats written-summary exercise builds data communication skill.

day.title: Sliding Window + Quantiles, IQR & Outliers
day.topics: Sliding window technique (fixed + variable), Kadane's algorithm, quartiles, percentiles, IQR, box plots, outlier detection methods, MAD, trimmed mean

concepts.concept: Sliding window technique: fixed-size windows, variable-size windows, templates
Key insight: sliding window avoids recomputation — maintain running state
Kadane's algorithm: maximum subarray sum in O(n)
Quantiles: quartiles (Q1, Q2, Q3), percentiles, deciles
IQR: Q3 - Q1 ("middle 50%"), resistant to outliers
Box plot anatomy: whiskers, box, median line, outlier dots
Outlier detection: IQR method (< Q1 - 1.5×IQR or > Q3 + 1.5×IQR), Z-score method (|z| > 3)
Robust statistics: Median Absolute Deviation (MAD), trimmed mean

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Sliding window theory: fixed-size and variable-size templates. Key insight: maintain running state. 7 problems: Maximum sum subarray of size k, Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters (LC #3-style), Minimum Size Subarray Sum (LC #209-style), Maximum Average Subarray (LC #643-style), count subarrays with sum equal to k (prefix-sum bridge to Day 27), Kadane's Algorithm for maximum subarray sum (LC #53-style), Fruit Into Baskets / longest substring with at most 2 distinct characters.

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Quantiles, IQR, outliers: quartiles/percentiles/deciles, IQR = Q3-Q1 (middle 50%). Box plot anatomy (whiskers, box, median, outlier dots). Outlier detection: IQR 1.5×rule, Z-score |z|>3. Robust stats: MAD, trimmed mean. Python: np.percentile, matplotlib boxplot, IQR detection from scratch. Exercise: load housing prices dataset, identify outliers using both methods, compare.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode Easy/Medium: 3 sliding window problems. StrataScratch: 1 Python question.
block.platform: LeetCode Easy/Medium (3 sliding window); StrataScratch (1 Python) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Review: fixed vs variable window templates. Stats: draw a box plot from memory and label all components.

day.notes: Kadane's is a FAANG-frequency problem — commit the pattern. Subarrays-sum-k foreshadows Day 27 prefix sums.

day.title: Hash Maps & Sets + Probability Foundations
day.topics: Hash map fundamentals (hashing, collision handling, O(1) average lookup), frequency counting, complement pattern, probability axioms, conditional probability, independence, Bayes' rule, base rate fallacy

concepts.concept: Hash map fundamentals: what hashing is, hash functions, collision handling (chaining, open addressing), O(1) average lookup
Python dicts and sets as hash maps: implementation under the hood
When to use hash maps: frequency counting, lookup optimization, complement problems
Sample space, events, probability axioms
Conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B), tree diagram intuition
Independence: P(A∩B) = P(A)·P(B), independence ≠ mutually exclusive
Bayes' rule: formula and intuition via medical-test example
Base rate fallacy: why P(disease|positive) differs wildly from test accuracy

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Hash map theory (hashing, collisions, chaining, open addressing, O(1) average). Python dict/set as hash maps. 8 problems: Two Sum with hash map (revisit from Day 22), Valid Anagram (LC #242-style), Group Anagrams (LC #49-style), Top K Frequent Elements (LC #347-style), Longest Consecutive Sequence (LC #128-style), Intersection of Two Arrays (LC #349-style), Subarray Sum Equals K using prefix sum + hash map (LC #560-style), Isomorphic Strings.

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Probability foundations: axioms, conditional probability P(A|B), independence, Bayes' rule. Medical test walkthrough (disease prevalence 1%, test accuracy 99% → P(disease|positive)) — base rate fallacy. Python: Bayes calculator, tree-diagram visualization. Exercise: 5 probability problems solved via both formula AND simulation (100k trials, compare to theoretical).

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode Easy/Medium: 3 hash map problems. DataLemur: 1 probability question.
block.platform: LeetCode (3 hash map); DataLemur (1 probability) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Review: O(n) brute-force → O(1) with hash map. Stats: recompute medical-test Bayes problem from memory.

day.notes: Subarray Sum = K (LC #560) bridges hash map + prefix sum (Day 27 topic). Bayes with tree diagrams is a key interview visual.

day.title: String Problems + Law of Total Probability & Independence
day.topics: String pattern problems (prefix, palindrome via expand-from-center, compression), Law of Total Probability, independence deep dive, uncorrelated ≠ independent

concepts.concept: String pattern problems building on Week 1 strings + Day 25 hash maps
Longest common prefix, string compression, valid parentheses (stack preview)
Expand-from-center technique for longest palindromic substring
Encode/decode with delimiters, count-and-say pattern
Law of Total Probability: P(A) = Σ P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ) — partition the sample space
Pairwise vs mutual independence
"Uncorrelated ≠ independent" — common interview trap

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: 8 string pattern problems: Longest Common Prefix (LC #14-style), String Compression ("aabcccaaa" → "a2b1c3a3"), Valid Parentheses stack-preview (LC #20-style) using list-as-stack, Implement strStr() (LC #28-style), Reverse Words in a String (LC #151-style), Longest Palindromic Substring expand-from-center (LC #5-style), Encode and Decode Strings (delimiter approach), Count and Say (LC #38-style).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Law of Total Probability: P(A) = Σ P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ) — partition the sample space. Intuition: "average the conditional probabilities, weighted by how likely each condition is." Example: customer churn across segments. Independence deep dive: pairwise vs mutual, "uncorrelated ≠ independent". Simulated examples: generate correlated and uncorrelated data. Exercise: 5 problems combining Bayes + LoTP. Python: build ProbabilityCalculator class handling conditional, Bayes, total probability.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode Medium: 3 string problems. StrataScratch: 1 Python question.
block.platform: LeetCode Medium (3 string); StrataScratch (1 Python) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Review: string patterns (prefix, palindrome, compression). Stats: derive Bayes' rule from the definition of conditional probability.

day.notes: Valid Parentheses here uses Python list-as-stack — explicit Stack class enters scope Day 36. Longest Palindromic Substring uses expand-from-center, NOT DP (DP is Phase 4).

day.title: Prefix Sums & Review + Probability Review
day.topics: Prefix sum technique (1D + 2D), range sum queries, week 4 DSA review, probability review via Monty Hall, Birthday problem, A/B false-positive preview

concepts.concept: 1D prefix sum: precompute cumulative sums for O(1) range queries
2D prefix sum: matrix region sums
Product of array except self (two-pass, no division)
Pivot index via prefix sums
Subarrays with sum divisible by k (prefix sum + modular arithmetic)
Monty Hall problem: intuition + simulation + Bayes connection
Birthday problem: collision probability + simulation
A/B testing false-positive preview: 5% significance → 5% false-positive rate

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Prefix sums deep dive: technique, when to use, template. 6 problems: Range Sum Query (LC #303-style), Product of Array Except Self (LC #238-style), Find Pivot Index (LC #724-style), 2D prefix sum (matrix region sum), Running Sum of 1D Array, Number of Subarrays with Sum Divisible by k. Plus: 5 timed mixed-pattern problems integrating two-pointer + sliding window + hash map from Week 4.

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Probability review integrating Days 25-26. Comprehensive exercise: Monty Hall — explain intuitively, prove with simulation, connect to Bayes. Birthday problem: P(2 of 23 share a birthday), simulate it. Applied: A/B testing preview — "5% significance → 5% false-positive rate". 10-question probability quiz (target ≥80%).

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 mixed problems (Easy + Medium). DataLemur: 2 questions.
block.platform: LeetCode Easy+Medium (3 mixed); DataLemur (2) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Week 4 review: what patterns felt hardest? (probably sliding window variable — that's normal). Stats: probability concept flowchart.

day.notes: First day with a scored assessment (probability quiz). Monty Hall demands BOTH simulation and formula. Total-per-day problem count (16) exceeds the standard-day cap (structure template will enforce at verification time).

day.title: Week 4 Review + Integration Day
day.topics: Review of Days 22-27 (Big-O, arrays, two-pointer, sliding window, hash maps, strings, prefix sums + descriptive stats, variance, quantiles/IQR, probability, Bayes, LoTP), self-assessment quiz, timed practice, Statistical Data Explorer integration project

concepts.concept: Review: all concepts from Days 22-27 (Week 4 DSA + Week 4 Stats)
Self-assessment: 20-question DSA quiz + 15-question Stats quiz
Timed problem-solving: 10 mixed problems in 3 hours
Integration: OOP wrapper for descriptive stats + outlier detection + visualization (DataExplorer class)

block.name: Morning Review
block.content: DSA review quiz (20 questions): hash map lookup complexity, two-pointer vs sliding window decision, prefix sum vs sliding window distinction, Two Sum from memory in O(n), Kadane's algorithm from memory, and 15 more. Stats review quiz (15 questions): population vs sample variance, median-vs-mean decision, P(A|B) from joint probability table, base rate fallacy in one sentence, and 11 more.

block.name: Midday Timed Practice
block.content: 10 problems in 3 hours (DSA + Stats combined): 5 DSA problems (mix of all Week 4 patterns), 3 probability computation problems, 2 StrataScratch/DataLemur questions.

block.name: Afternoon Integration
block.content: Integration project: Statistical Data Explorer. Load any CSV/JSON dataset. Automatically compute descriptive statistics (mean, median, mode, variance, std, quantiles, IQR). Detect and flag outliers (IQR + Z-score methods). Generate a summary report. Visualize distributions (histograms, box plots). Uses OOP (class DataExplorer), error handling, modular code. Push to GitHub.

block.name: Evening Reflection
block.content: Reflect on Week 4 hybrid (DSA + Stats) schedule. Is the pace sustainable? Adjust if needed. Preview Week 5: Sorting algorithms + Distributions.

checkpoint.criterion: Can analyze any code snippet's complexity in < 1 min

criterion.test: Two-pointer, sliding window, hash map patterns mastered

checkpoint.criterion: Timed problem set: 5/7 correct in time

criterion.test: Descriptive statistics implemented from scratch + with libraries

checkpoint.criterion: 15+ Easy problems solved, first Medium attempts

day.notes: Review Day — no new concepts. Hybrid quiz format (35 total questions: 20 DSA + 15 Stats) differs from Phase 1's 20-question single quiz. DataExplorer is Week 4 deliverable (Checkpoint gate).

week.title: Sorting & Searching + Distributions & Sampling
week.objective: Implement sorting algorithms from scratch (bubble/selection/ insertion + merge sort + quicksort), master binary search (3 templates + binary search on answer), learn probability distributions (Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Normal, Exponential) + CLT + Standard Error + sampling. Solve two target problems: Largest Perimeter (Problem #15) and Sieve of Eratosthenes (Problem #6).

day.title: Basic Sorting Algorithms + Bernoulli & Binomial
day.topics: Bubble sort, selection sort, insertion sort, stability, Bernoulli distribution, Binomial distribution, A/B testing connection

concepts.concept: Why learn sorting from scratch: understand trade-offs, interview fundamentals, foundation for harder problems
Bubble sort: O(n²), optimized with early termination
Selection sort: O(n²), always same comparisons regardless of input
Insertion sort: O(n²) worst, O(n) best (nearly-sorted), Timsort base case
Comparison: when each sort is appropriate
Stability: what it means and why it matters
Bernoulli distribution: P(success)=p, mean=p, variance=p(1-p)
Binomial distribution: n independent Bernoulli trials, count successes, mean=np, variance=np(1-p), PMF formula
Connection to A/B testing: binomial underpins proportion comparison

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Three basic sorts: Bubble (with early-termination optimization), Selection (fixed comparisons), Insertion (O(n) best for nearly-sorted, used as Timsort base). Comparison table (time, space, stability, best use). 4 problems: implement all three and test on random arrays, count comparisons and swaps on same input, sort list of tuples by second element (test stability), insertion sort on linked list (conceptual only — full LL is Phase 3).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Bernoulli distribution: single trial with P(success)=p, mean=p, variance=p(1-p). Real-world: coin flip, click/no-click, conversion. scipy.stats.bernoulli: simulate 10,000 Bernoulli trials. Binomial distribution: n independent trials, PMF formula, mean=np, variance=np(1-p). Plot PMF for different n, p. Connection to A/B testing. Exercise: "1000 flips, 530 heads — is the coin fair?" (hypothesis testing preview).

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 sorting-related problems. Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag) revisited with sorting lens. Merge Sorted Array (LC #88-style). Valid Anagram sorting vs hash approach.
block.platform: LeetCode (3 sorting: Sort Colors, Merge Sorted Array #88, Valid Anagram) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Review sort comparison table. Stats: draw Binomial PMF for n=20, p=0.3 from intuition.

day.notes: scipy.stats enters scope today — first new library of Phase 2. Use np.random.seed() for reproducibility in simulations.

day.title: Merge Sort & Quicksort + Poisson Distribution
day.topics: Merge sort (divide and conquer), quicksort (Lomuto/Hoare partitions, pivot selection), Timsort, Poisson distribution, Binomial→Poisson limit

concepts.concept: Merge sort: divide step, recursive calls, merge step; O(n log n) always, O(n) space; stable
Quicksort: partition step (Lomuto vs Hoare), pivot selection (first, last, random, median-of-three), O(n log n) avg / O(n²) worst; in-place, unstable
Python's sorted() and .sort(): Timsort (hybrid merge + insertion), why Python chose it
Kth Largest Element via quickselect (O(n) average)
Poisson distribution: models number of events in a fixed interval, parameter λ, mean=variance=λ
Rare-events intuition: "known average rate"
Binomial → Poisson limit: when n is large and p is small, Binomial(n,p) ≈ Poisson(np)

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Merge sort deep dive: divide/recurse/merge, draw the full tree, implement from scratch. O(n log n) always, O(n) space. Stable, predictable, good for linked lists + external sorting. Quicksort deep dive: partition (Lomuto vs Hoare), pivot selection (random, median-of-three). O(n log n) avg / O(n²) worst (sorted input + bad pivot). In-place, unstable. Comparison: merge vs quick. Python Timsort overview. 4 problems: merge sort for strings, quicksort with random pivot tested on sorted input, sort list of dicts by key with comparison function, Kth Largest Element via quickselect (LC #215-style).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Poisson distribution: "number of events in a fixed interval" when independent and rate constant. Parameter λ, mean=variance=λ. PMF formula + intuition. Real-world: tickets per hour, errors per day, customers per minute. Binomial→Poisson limit: large n, small p → Binomial(n,p) ≈ Poisson(np), show via simulation. scipy.stats.poisson PMF plot for different λ. Exercise: "3 errors/day average — P(0 errors tomorrow)? P(5 or more)?" Calculate + simulate.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 2 sorting-based problems. DataLemur: 1 statistics question.
block.platform: LeetCode (2 sorting-based); DataLemur (1 stats) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Implement merge sort from scratch in ≤15 minutes. Stats: Poisson vs Binomial decision rule.

day.notes: Quickselect is a stretch (O(n) average). Recursion is already in scope from Phase 1 (Day 15-16) — no new scope for recursive algorithms.

day.title: Binary Search + Normal Distribution & CLT
day.topics: Binary search (three templates: exact, leftmost, rightmost), common pitfalls, Normal distribution, 68-95-99.7 rule, Standard Normal, Central Limit Theorem, Standard Error

concepts.concept: Binary search fundamentals: sorted data, halving search space, O(log n)
Three templates: find exact, find leftmost (bisect_left-style), find rightmost (bisect_right-style)
Common pitfalls: off-by-one errors, infinite loops, integer overflow (not in Python but know it)
Search in Rotated Sorted Array, Find Peak Element, Sqrt(x) via binary search
Normal (Gaussian) distribution: bell curve, parameters μ and σ
Standard Normal: Z = (X - μ)/σ, Z-tables conceptually
PDF formula awareness (understand shape, don't memorize)
Central Limit Theorem: sampling distribution of the mean approaches Normal regardless of underlying distribution
Standard Error: SE = σ/√n, standard deviation of the sampling distribution

methods_introduced.method: binary_search(arr, target) — exact, leftmost, rightmost variants

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Binary search theory: sorted data, halving search space, O(log n). Three templates: exact, leftmost, rightmost. Common pitfalls. 8 problems: Standard binary search, Search Insert Position (LC #35), First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array (LC #34), Search in Rotated Sorted Array (LC #33), Find Peak Element (LC #162), Sqrt(x) via binary search (LC #69), Search a 2D Matrix (LC #74), Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array (LC #153).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Normal distribution: bell curve, μ + σ parameters. 68-95-99.7 rule — draw it, internalize. Standard Normal: Z = (X-μ)/σ. PDF formula + shape. scipy.stats.norm for PDF/CDF plotting. Central Limit Theorem: sampling distribution of mean → Normal regardless of source. Demonstrate with simulation from 3 non-normal sources (uniform, exponential, Poisson). Standard Error: SE = σ/√n. Exercise: simulate CLT with 3 different non-normal distributions.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 binary search Medium problems. StrataScratch: 1 question.
block.platform: LeetCode Medium (3 binary search); StrataScratch (1) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Write all three binary search templates from memory. Stats: explain CLT without jargon.

day.notes: CLT is the foundational theorem for Week 6's confidence intervals and hypothesis testing. Simulation approach is mandatory for intuition.

day.title: Binary Search Advanced + Exponential Distribution
day.topics: Binary search on answer (monotonic answer space), bisect module, Exponential distribution, memoryless property, distribution relationship diagram

concepts.concept: Binary search on answer: when the answer space is monotonic, binary search on the answer itself
Capacity to Ship Packages, Koko Eating Bananas, Split Array Largest Sum patterns
Find the duplicate number via binary search
Median of Two Sorted Arrays (hard stretch)
bisect module: bisect_left, bisect_right, insort — when to use the library
Exponential distribution: models time between events (continuous counterpart to Poisson), parameter λ, mean=1/λ
Memoryless property: P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t) — "future doesn't depend on time already waited"
Distribution relationships: Bernoulli → Binomial, Poisson ↔ Exponential, Binomial → Normal (CLT), Binomial → Poisson (large n, small p)

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Binary search on answer: when answer space is monotonic, BS on the answer itself. 6 problems: Capacity to Ship Packages (LC #1011), Koko Eating Bananas (LC #875), Split Array Largest Sum (LC #410, hard — doable with BS on answer), Find the Duplicate Number (LC #287, BS approach), Median of Two Sorted Arrays (LC #4, stretch), bisect module tour (bisect_left / bisect_right / insort).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Exponential distribution: time between events (continuous counterpart to Poisson). Parameter λ, mean=1/λ, memoryless property. Real-world: customer arrivals, failure times, session duration. Memoryless: P(X>s+t | X>s) = P(X>t). scipy.stats.expon PDF/CDF/ survival function. Distribution relationships diagram: Bernoulli → Binomial → Normal (CLT) / Poisson (large n, small p); Poisson ↔ Exponential. Exercise: given a real dataset, identify best-fit distribution via histograms + goodness-of-fit.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 2 binary-search-on-answer problems. DataLemur: 2 questions.

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Recognize binary-search-on-answer pattern. Stats: draw distribution relationship diagram from memory.

day.notes: bisect module enters scope today. Median of Two Sorted Arrays is LC Hard — mark as optional stretch.

day.title: Mixed Sorting/Searching + Sampling
day.topics: Largest Perimeter triangle (greedy + sorting), Merge Intervals, Meeting Rooms II (sweep line), H-Index, custom sort keys, sampling methods, sampling bias, bootstrap Standard Error

concepts.concept: Largest Perimeter: sort descending, check triangle inequality b+c > a, first valid triple wins — greedy + sorting
Merge Intervals: sort by start, then merge
Meeting Rooms II: how many rooms needed — sweep line approach (heap approach reserved for Day 40)
H-Index via sort
Custom sort keys: sort by frequency then by value
Sampling methods: simple random, stratified, cluster, systematic — when to use each
Sampling bias: selection bias, survivorship bias, response bias, convenience sampling — real examples
Representativeness: why your sample must represent the population
Point estimates vs uncertainty: "mean is 42" vs "mean is 42 ± 3"
Standard Error intuition: "how much would the estimate change on a different sample?" SE = σ/√n — √n relationship
Bootstrap Standard Error from scratch

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Largest Perimeter Triangle (TARGET PROBLEM #15): sort descending, for each triple (a,b,c) where a≥b≥c check b+c>a, first valid triple gives largest perimeter. Greedy + sorting. Implement from scratch, verify against LC #976. O(n log n). 5 mixed sort/search problems: Merge Intervals (LC #56, sort + merge), Meeting Rooms II (sweep-line approach today), array partitioning, H-Index (sort-based), custom sort (sort by frequency then value).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Sampling and Estimation: simple random/stratified/cluster/ systematic sampling. Sampling biases: selection, survivorship, response, convenience. Representativeness. Point estimates vs uncertainty. SE intuition: "different sample → different estimate". SE = σ/√n: why √n? Intuition via simulation — plot SE as n grows. Python: simulate biased sampling showing distortion. Implement bootstrap SE from scratch. Exercise: 10,000 customer purchases — estimate mean with uncertainty for n ∈ {100, 500, 1000, 5000}.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 mixed problems (sorting + searching). StrataScratch: 1 question.
block.platform: LeetCode (3 mixed sort/search); StrataScratch (1) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Largest Perimeter from scratch in ≤5 minutes. Stats: name 4 sampling biases with real examples.

day.notes: TARGET PROBLEM #15 (Largest Perimeter) focus day. Meeting Rooms II uses sweep-line today — heap approach reserved for Day 40 when heapq enters scope.

day.title: Sieve of Eratosthenes + Standard Error Deep Dive
day.topics: Sieve of Eratosthenes, primality (O(√n)), prime factorization, GCD/LCM, fast exponentiation (O(log n)), Palindrome Number, Roman ↔ integer, Standard Error deep dive (mean/proportion/difference), margin of error, sample size derivation, bootstrap SE

concepts.concept: Sieve of Eratosthenes: mark multiples of p starting from p², remaining are primes; O(n log log n)
Primality testing via O(√n) trial division
Prime factorization, GCD (Euclidean), LCM = (a*b)/GCD
Pow(x, n) via O(log n) fast exponentiation (recursion halving)
Palindrome Number without string conversion
Roman numeral conversion (Roman to Integer, Integer to Roman)
Standard Error for different estimators: SE of mean, SE of proportion, SE of difference
Margin of error: how it relates to confidence intervals (Day 36 preview)
Sample size and SE: √n relationship — quadrupling n halves SE
Bootstrap SE: when theoretical SE unknown or formula doesn't exist — resample with replacement, compute statistic, SE = std of bootstrapped values
Sample size derivation for target margin of error

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Sieve of Eratosthenes (TARGET PROBLEM #6): step-by-step implementation, optimization (start from p², only check up to √N). O(n log log n). Space O(n). Variations: segmented sieve for very large N, counting primes. 4 number theory bonus: Count Primes below N (LC #204), primality O(√n), prime factorization, GCD (Euclidean) + LCM. 3 math-pattern: Pow(x, n) via O(log n) fast exponentiation (LC #50), Palindrome Number (LC #9), Roman to Integer / Integer to Roman.

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Standard Error deep dive: SE of mean, SE of proportion, SE of difference. Margin of error → confidence intervals preview. √n relationship. Bootstrap SE: resample with replacement 10,000×, compute statistic, SE = std. Implement from scratch. Practical: compute SE of mean via formula AND bootstrap on real dataset, compare. Sample size derivation: "how large a sample for mean ±2 at 95% confidence?" Interview connection: "why is sample size important in A/B testing?" → SE → power (Day 38).

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 2 math problems + 1 array problem. DataLemur: 1 question.
block.platform: LeetCode (2 math + 1 array); DataLemur (1) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Sieve from scratch in ≤5 minutes. Stats: SE formula and intuition, no lookup.

day.notes: TARGET PROBLEM #6 (Sieve of Eratosthenes) focus day. Fast exponentiation uses recursion from Phase 1 Days 15-16 — no new scope.

day.title: Week 5 Review + Integration Day
day.topics: Review of Days 29-34 (sorting, binary search, sieve + Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Normal, Exponential, CLT, SE), self-assessment quiz, timed practice, Distribution Explorer integration project

concepts.concept: Review: all Week 5 DSA (basic sorts, merge sort, quicksort, binary search 3 templates + BS on answer, Sieve)
Review: all Week 5 Stats (5 distributions: Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Normal, Exponential; CLT; Standard Error)
Self-assessment: 20-question DSA quiz + 15-question Stats quiz
Timed problem-solving: 10 mixed problems in 3 hours
Integration: Distribution Explorer demonstrating all 5 distributions + CLT + bootstrap CIs

block.name: Morning Review
block.content: DSA quiz (20 questions): merge sort time and space complexity, when quicksort is O(n²), write leftmost binary search from memory, identify the BS-on-answer pattern, implement the Sieve from memory, and 15 more. Stats quiz (15 questions): Binomial(100, 0.3) mean and variance, CLT in one sentence, memoryless property and which distribution has it, distribution relationship diagram, SE if σ=10 and n=100, and 10 more.

block.name: Midday Timed Practice
block.content: 10 problems in 3 hours: 5 DSA (sorting/searching mix), 3 statistics computation problems, 2 platform questions (StrataScratch/DataLemur).

block.name: Afternoon Integration
block.content: Integration project: Distribution Explorer (class or Jupyter notebook). Generate samples from any of the 5 distributions (Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Normal, Exponential). Compute and display descriptive statistics. Demonstrate CLT (take means of samples, show convergence to Normal). Calculate bootstrap CIs and SE. Visualize with matplotlib. Push to GitHub.

block.name: Evening Reflection
block.content: Week 5 reflection: are sorting algorithms solid? Can you implement all 5 distributions in Python? Preview Week 6: stacks, queues, hypothesis testing.

criterion.test: Can implement merge sort and quicksort from scratch
CLT and SE understood intuitively + mathematically

checkpoint.criterion: Can explain to a non-technical audience

day.notes: Review Day — no new concepts. Hybrid quiz format (35 questions). Distribution Explorer is Week 5 deliverable — CLT demonstration is the showcase piece.

week.title: Stacks, Queues, Heaps + Confidence Intervals & Hypothesis Testing
week.objective: Implement stacks and queues from scratch, master classic applications (balanced parentheses, min-stack, monotonic stack), implement heaps and priority queues via heapq, complete hypothesis testing foundation (CIs, Type I/II errors, power, t-test, proportion z-test, chi-square). Solve 4 target problems this week (Stack, Balancing Special Text, Min-Stack/Monotonic-Stack, Matrix Multiplication).

day.title: Stack Implementation + Confidence Intervals
day.topics: Stack ADT (LIFO), three implementations (list, simplified linked-list preview, fixed-size array), stack applications overview, confidence intervals (frequentist interpretation), bootstrap CI

concepts.concept: Stack concept: LIFO (Last In, First Out) — push, pop, peek, is_empty, size
Implementation 1: Python list (simple abstraction, O(1) amortized)
Implementation 2: Linked list (simplified Node preview — full LL is Phase 3)
Implementation 3: Fixed-size array stack (for understanding overflow)
Stack applications overview: expression evaluation, undo/redo, browser back/forward, DFS
Confidence interval: "if we repeated the experiment 100 times, ~95 of intervals would contain the true parameter" — NOT "there's a 95% chance the true value is in this interval"
3 common CI misconceptions (list and correct each)
CI for mean: x̄ ± z*(σ/√n) for large n, x̄ ± t*(s/√n) for small n
CI for proportion: p̂ ± z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n)
CI width depends on confidence level (z*), variability (σ), sample size (n)
Bootstrap CI: percentile method (2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of bootstrapped stat)
BCa method (bias-corrected and accelerated) via scipy.stats.bootstrap

methods_introduced.method: Stack class (list-backed: push, pop, peek, is_empty, size, __str__)

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Stack concept: LIFO. 3 implementations: Python list (O(1) amortized), simplified linked list (Node class preview — full LL is Phase 3), fixed-size array (overflow awareness). Stack applications: expression eval, undo/redo, browser history, DFS. 5 problems: Stack class (push/pop/peek/is_empty/size/__str__), reverse a string using stack, check if push/pop sequence is valid, Queue via Two Stacks (LC #232), Stack via Two Queues (LC #225). TARGET PROBLEM #3 (Stack): implementation focus today; min-stack + monotonic patterns on Day 38.

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Confidence intervals: correct frequentist interpretation + 3 common misconceptions. Formula CI for mean (z* large n, t* small n), CI for proportion. Width drivers: confidence level, σ, n. Bootstrap CI: percentile method from scratch, BCa via scipy.stats.bootstrap. Python: compute CIs manually and with scipy. Exercise: 50 customer satisfaction scores — compute 95% CI for the MEDIAN (formula-based approach fails; bootstrap works).

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 stack Easy problems. StrataScratch: 1 question.
block.platform: LeetCode Easy (3 stack); StrataScratch (1) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Draw stack state after 5 operations. Stats: correct the 3 common CI misconceptions.

day.notes: TARGET PROBLEM #3 (Stack) — implementation focus today; min-stack + monotonic stack patterns continue Day 38. Linked-list implementation is SIMPLIFIED (Node class with value + next, push/pop only) — full LL operations are Phase 3 (Week 7). Explicit Stack class convention starts today (replaces list-as-stack from Day 26).

day.title: Balanced Parentheses & Stack Applications + Hypothesis Testing Framework
day.topics: Balanced parentheses (multiple delimiter types), stack applications (RPN evaluation, monotonic stack introduction via Daily Temperatures, Next Greater Element, Decode String, Simplify Path), hypothesis testing framework (H₀/H₁, p-value, α, decision rule)

concepts.concept: Balanced Parentheses — classic stack problem: check ()[]{} balancing
Algorithm: push opening delimiters, pop and match closing
Edge cases: empty string, only closing, mismatched types, extra opening
Extensions: wildcards, custom delimiters, nested HTML tags
RPN (Reverse Polish Notation) evaluation
Monotonic stack introduction via Daily Temperatures
Next Greater Element pattern
Decode String (nested bracket decoding)
Simplify Path (filesystem-style path canonicalization)
Hypothesis testing logic: assume H₀, measure surprise
H₀ (null hypothesis) vs H₁ (alternative hypothesis), one-tailed vs two-tailed
p-value: probability under H₀ of data this extreme or more — NOT the probability H₀ is true
Significance level α (typically 0.05) — threshold for "surprising enough"
Decision rule: if p < α, reject H₀; otherwise, fail to reject (NOT "accept H₀")
Simulation-based hypothesis testing: generate null distribution via permutation (10,000 trials)

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Balanced Parentheses (TARGET PROBLEM #1): multi-delimiter version with ()[]{}. Extensions: wildcards, custom delimiters, nested HTML tags. Algorithm: push opens, pop-and-match on closes. Edge cases. 6 stack applications: Valid Parentheses (LC #20) full solution, Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation (LC #150), Daily Temperatures (LC #739) — monotonic stack introduction, Next Greater Element (LC #496), Decode String (LC #394), Simplify Path (LC #71).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Hypothesis testing framework. Logic: assume H₀, quantify surprise. H₀ vs H₁, one-tailed vs two-tailed. p-value definition (common misinterpretations!). α significance level. Decision rule. Python: implement hypothesis test from scratch via simulation — 1) state H₀/H₁, 2) choose test statistic, 3) generate null distribution via 10,000 permutations, 4) compute p-value, 5) decide. Exercise: "Coin flipped 100 times, lands heads 60. Is it fair?" Solve with simulation AND formula.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 stack Easy/Medium. DataLemur: 1 statistics question.
block.platform: LeetCode Easy/Medium (3 stack); DataLemur (1 stats) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Balanced Parens in ≤5 minutes. Stats: hypothesis test decision flowchart from memory.

day.notes: TARGET PROBLEM #1 (Balancing Special Text). Monotonic stack introduced today (Daily Temperatures); deepened Day 38.

day.title: Advanced Stack Patterns + Type I/II Errors & Power
day.topics: Min-Stack (2 approaches), monotonic stack pattern, Largest Rectangle in Histogram (stack classic), Trapping Rain Water (stack), Online Stock Span, Remove K Digits, Asteroid Collision, Type I / Type II errors, statistical power, power analysis

concepts.concept: Min-Stack: approach 1 — two stacks (main + min-tracking)
Min-Stack: approach 2 — store (value, current_min) tuples
Monotonic stack pattern: maintaining increasing/decreasing order for efficient next-greater/smaller lookup
Largest Rectangle in Histogram — monotonic stack classic (LC Hard)
Trapping Rain Water — stack approach + precomputed-heights approach
Online Stock Span, Remove K Digits, Asteroid Collision
Type I error (false positive): rejecting H₀ when it's true — probability = α
Type II error (false negative): failing to reject H₀ when H₁ is true — probability = β
α/β trade-off: decreasing α increases β; can't minimize both
Real-world framing: Type I = "launching a feature that doesn't help"; Type II = "not launching a feature that does"
Statistical power: Power = 1 - β — probability of correctly detecting a real effect
Power drivers: sample size (↑n → ↑power), effect size, α, variability (↓σ → ↑power)
Power analysis: "How many samples for 80% power at 5% α to detect a 2% lift?"

methods_introduced.method: MinStack class (TARGET PROBLEM #3: two-stack variant + tuple variant)

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Min-Stack (TARGET PROBLEM #3 continued): approach 1 two-stacks (main + min-tracking), approach 2 tuple storage. Space trade-off analysis. LC #155. Monotonic Stack pattern: increasing/decreasing templates. Algorithm walkthrough. 6 problems: Min Stack (LC #155), Largest Rectangle in Histogram (LC #84, monotonic classic, LC Hard — don't skip), Trapping Rain Water (LC #42, stack approach), Online Stock Span (LC #901), Remove K Digits (LC #402), Asteroid Collision (LC #735).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Type I/II errors. α/β trade-off. Real-world framing (medical tests vs marketing). Statistical Power = 1 - β. Power drivers: n, effect size, α, σ. Power analysis: statsmodels.stats.power.TTestIndPower. Simulation: generate data with known effect, run test 10,000 times, count detections = empirical power. Exercise: A/B test design — current conversion 10%, detect 2% absolute increase at 80% power, 5% α. How many users per group?

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 2 monotonic stack Medium problems. StrataScratch: 1 question.
block.platform: LeetCode Medium (2 monotonic stack); StrataScratch (1) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Min-stack and monotonic stack templates. Stats: hand-compute power-analysis sample size.

day.notes: TARGET PROBLEM #3 continued (min-stack + monotonic patterns). statsmodels enters scope today — first and only Phase 2 day introducing statsmodels power functions. Largest Rectangle in Histogram is LC Hard but canonical monotonic-stack — do not skip.

day.title: Queues & Deques + T-test & Proportion Test
day.topics: Queue ADT (FIFO), three implementations (list — inefficient, collections.deque — efficient, circular queue with fixed-size array), deque double-ended operations, Sliding Window Maximum (monotonic deque), t-test variants (1-sample, 2-sample Welch/pooled, paired), proportion z-test, assumption checks (Shapiro-Wilk, Levene)

concepts.concept: Queue concept: FIFO (First In, First Out) — enqueue, dequeue, front, is_empty
Implementation 1: Python list (O(n) for dequeue — inefficient, explain why)
Implementation 2: collections.deque (O(1) both ends — efficient, already in scope from Phase 1 Day 20 but now used as queue)
Implementation 3: Circular queue via fixed-size array (head/tail pointers)
Deque (double-ended queue): operations at both ends
Sliding Window Maximum via monotonic deque (LC Hard but canonical)
One-sample t-test: "is this sample mean different from a known value?"
Two-sample t-test (independent): Welch's (unequal variance) vs pooled (equal variance)
Paired t-test: "did the treatment change things for the same subjects?"
t-distribution: heavier tails than Normal, approaches Normal as df → ∞
When to use t-test vs z-test: small n + unknown σ → t-test; large n → either works
Proportion z-test: one-sample and two-sample for comparing conversion rates
Assumption checks: Shapiro-Wilk (normality), Levene's test (equal variance)

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Queue concept: FIFO. 3 implementations: Python list (inefficient, explain why O(n)), collections.deque (O(1) both ends), circular queue via fixed-size array. Deque operations at both ends. 6 problems: Queue class (deque-backed), Circular Queue (LC #622), Number of Recent Calls (LC #933), Sliding Window Maximum (LC #239, monotonic deque, LC Hard), Design Hit Counter, Round Robin scheduler concept.

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: T-test variants: 1-sample, 2-sample independent (Welch vs pooled — equal-variance assumption), paired. t-distribution heavier tails, → Normal as df grows. When t vs z. Python: scipy.stats.ttest_1samp / ttest_ind / ttest_rel. Proportion z-test via statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportions_ztest. Assumption checks: scipy.stats.shapiro (normality), scipy.stats. levene (equal variance). Exercise: Group A: 500 users, 60 converted. Group B: 500 users, 75 converted. Full test with all steps.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 queue problems. DataLemur: 1 question.

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Circular Queue index math. Stats: T-test decision tree — which variant when?

day.notes: Sliding Window Maximum is LC Hard but canonical. collections.deque usage broadens from Phase 1 Day 20 (general-purpose) to explicit queue semantics today.

day.title: Priority Queue / Heaps + Chi-Square Test
day.topics: Heap ADT (complete binary tree, min-heap / max-heap property), Python heapq module, priority queue pattern, max-heap via negation, chi-square goodness of fit, chi-square test of independence, contingency tables, degrees of freedom, Fisher's exact test

concepts.concept: Heap: complete binary tree, heap-order property, array representation
Operations: insert (bubble up), extract-min (bubble down), heapify
heapq module: heappush, heappop, heapify, nlargest, nsmallest
Python has min-heap only — negate values for max-heap behavior
Priority queue = heap underneath
Kth Largest in Stream, Last Stone Weight (max-heap via negation), K Closest Points to Origin
Merge K Sorted Lists (concept only — full implementation after Phase 3 linked lists)
Find Median from Data Stream via two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Chi-square goodness of fit: "does this distribution match what we expected?"
Chi-square test of independence: "are these two categorical variables related?"
Contingency tables: construct, compute expected frequencies, χ² statistic
Degrees of freedom: (rows - 1) × (cols - 1) for independence test
Assumption: expected frequency ≥ 5 per cell; otherwise use Fisher's exact test

methods_introduced.method: MinHeap class (from scratch, array-based: insert, extract_min, heapify_up, heapify_down)

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Heap: complete binary tree, heap-order property, array representation. Operations: insert (bubble up), extract-min (bubble down), heapify. heapq module tour: heappush, heappop, heapify, nlargest, nsmallest. Python is min-heap only — negate for max-heap. Priority Queue = heap underneath. 6 problems: MinHeap from scratch (array-based), Kth Largest Element in a Stream (LC #703), Last Stone Weight (LC #1046) via max-heap- by-negation, K Closest Points to Origin (LC #973), Merge K Sorted Lists (LC #23 concept only), Find Median from Data Stream (LC #295, two-heaps approach).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Chi-square goodness of fit: "does this distribution match expected?" Example: are website visits equally distributed across weekdays? Chi-square test of independence: "are these two categorical variables related?" Example: user plan (free/paid) vs churn (yes/no). Contingency tables, expected frequencies, χ² statistic. Degrees of freedom: (r-1)(c-1). Python: scipy.stats.chisquare, scipy.stats.chi2_contingency. Assumption: expected freq ≥ 5 per cell → else scipy.stats.fisher_exact. Exercise: click data across 4 button colors and 3 user segments. Is preference independent of segment?

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 2 heap Medium problems. StrataScratch: 1 question.
block.platform: LeetCode Medium (2 heap); StrataScratch (1) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Draw heap array/tree representation and trace insert + extract. Stats: chi-square variant decision rule (GoF vs independence).

day.notes: heapq enters scope today. Merge K Sorted Lists is conceptual only — full implementation deferred to Phase 3 (Week 7) after linked lists.

day.title: Mixed Stack/Queue Practice + Matrix Multiplication + Stats Integration
day.topics: Matrix multiplication from scratch (triple nested loop), mixed stack/queue problems (MaxStack, stack-with-middle, Backspace String Compare, Next Greater Element II circular, Remove Adjacent Duplicates), hypothesis testing integration (test selection decision framework), multiple comparisons preview, statistical vs practical significance

concepts.concept: Matrix multiplication: for A (m×n) and B (n×p), C (m×p) where C[i][j] = Σ A[i][k] × B[k][j]
Triple nested loop implementation — O(m·n·p), O(n³) for square
Verification via NumPy: A @ B or np.dot(A, B)
Strassen's algorithm O(n^2.807) exists (mention only, no implementation)
Edge cases: non-square matrices, incompatible dimensions, 1D vectors
MaxStack: push, pop, peekMax, popMax (O(1) each)
Stack with getMiddle operation
Backspace String Compare: stack-based and two-pointer variants
Next Greater Element II (circular array, monotonic stack)
Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String
Hypothesis test selection decision framework: comparing 1 group to a value / 2 groups continuous / paired / proportions / categories
Multiple comparisons preview: testing many things inflates false-positive rate — p-hacking risk
Statistical significance ≠ practical significance: large n can make tiny differences "significant"

block.name: DSA Morning
block.content: Matrix Multiplication from scratch (TARGET PROBLEM #13): C[i][j] = Σ A[i][k] × B[k][j]. Triple nested loop O(m·n·p). Verify with NumPy @ and np.dot. Complexity: O(n³) for square; Strassen O(n^2.807) mention only. Edge cases: non-square, dim mismatch, vectors. 5 mixed stack/queue problems: MaxStack (push/pop/peekMax/popMax), stack with getMiddle, Backspace String Compare (LC #844, stack + two-pointer), Next Greater Element II (LC #503, circular monotonic), Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String (LC #1047).

block.name: Stats Midday
block.content: Hypothesis Testing Integration: decision framework. "I have data — which test?" 1 group vs value → 1-sample t-test; 2 groups continuous → 2-sample t-test / Mann-Whitney (Phase 3); paired → paired t-test / Wilcoxon (Phase 3); proportions → proportion z-test; categories → chi-square. 5 scenario-based problems: identify test, state H₀/H₁, check assumptions, run in Python, interpret in plain English. Common pitfalls: multiple comparisons / p-hacking, statistical vs practical significance.

block.name: Problem Practice
block.content: LeetCode: 3 mixed problems, timed (20 min each). DataLemur: 1 question.
block.platform: LeetCode timed (3 mixed, 20 min each); DataLemur (1) [verify on platform]

block.name: Evening Review
block.content: Matrix multiplication from scratch in ≤10 minutes. Stats: commit test selection flowchart to memory.

day.notes: TARGET PROBLEM #13 (Matrix Multiplication) focus day. Matrix mult verification against `A @ B` (numpy) is mandatory. Integration session — no NEW stat tests today. Mann-Whitney / Wilcoxon (non-parametric) are Phase 3 Week 7 — mentioned as "next week" preview only.

day.title: Week 6 Review + Applied Hypothesis Testing Project
day.topics: Review of Days 36-41 (stacks, queues, heaps, matrix mult + CIs, hypothesis testing framework, Type I/II, power, t-tests, proportion z-test, chi-square), self-assessment quiz, timed practice, A/B Test Analyzer v1 integration project, Phase 2 retrospective

concepts.concept: Review: all Week 6 DSA (stack list + min + monotonic; queue list/deque/circular; heap/heapq; matrix mult)
Review: all Week 6 Stats (CIs + bootstrap CI; hypothesis test framework; Type I/II; power; t-tests; proportion z-test; chi-square GoF and independence)
Self-assessment: 20-question DSA quiz + 15-question Stats quiz
Timed problem-solving: 10 mixed problems in 3 hours
Integration: A/B Test Analyzer v1 — auto-selects test, runs it, reports results in plain English
Phase 2 retrospective: target problems solved, DSA + Stats coverage, LeetCode count

block.name: Morning Review
block.content: DSA quiz (20 questions): implement a stack's push and pop from memory; min-stack getMin complexity; trace monotonic stack on [3,1,4,1,5,9]; stack vs queue; matrix multiplication for 2×3 and 3×2 from memory; heap vs sorted list; and 14 more. Stats quiz (15 questions): what a p-value of 0.03 means in plain English; Type I vs Type II; paired vs independent t-test; 95% CI for a sample mean given data; statistical power and its drivers; and 10 more.

block.name: Midday Timed Practice
block.content: 10 problems in 3 hours: 5 DSA (stacks, queues, heaps, mixed), 3 hypothesis testing problems, 2 platform questions.

block.name: Afternoon Integration
block.content: Integration project: A/B Test Analyzer (v1). Input: two groups of data (from CSV or generated). Automatically: 1) compute descriptive statistics per group, 2) visualize distributions, 3) select appropriate test (t-test / proportion z-test / chi-square), 4) run the test and report p-value, CI, effect size, 5) make recommendation in plain English. Uses OOP (ABTestAnalyzer class), proper error handling. Explicitly v1 — full version with power analysis + CUPED + Bayesian option is Capstone 1 in Weeks 10-12 (Phase 4). Push to GitHub.

block.name: Evening Reflection
block.content: Phase 2 retrospective: 6 target problems now solvable (Nested JSON Flattening carried from Day 20, Sieve, Largest Perimeter, Balancing Special Text, Stack, Matrix Multiplication). DSA: arrays, strings, hashing, sorting, searching, stacks, queues, heaps. Stats: all of Tier 1A through hypothesis testing (non-parametric tests arrive Phase 3 Week 7). LeetCode: should be 50+ problems by now.

criterion.test: Chi-square, t-test, proportion test all implemented

day.notes: Review Day — no new concepts. Week 6 AND Phase 2 retrospective. A/B Test Analyzer is explicitly v1 — full version (power analysis, CUPED, Bayesian) is Capstone 1 in Phase 4. Phase 3 (Week 7) begins linked lists + non-parametric tests at T2.

problem.name: Prime Numbers Below N (Sieve of Eratosthenes)

platform.name: LeetCode
platform.sections: Easy array / two-pointer / sliding window / hash map / strings / prefix sums (Week 4); sorting / binary search / number theory (Week 5); stack / queue / heap / matrix (Week 6) — Easy through Hard

platform.name: StrataScratch
platform.sections: Python Easy and Medium (continued from Phase 1)

platform.name: DataLemur
platform.sections: Python + probability + statistics Easy and Medium

platform.name: HackerRank
platform.sections: Continued Phase 1 usage (Python Introduction, Strings, Lists, Collections)
